segments.text: Good afternoon.
Welcome to the Social Services Community Committee.
Uh, thank you for coming on this afternoon.
So, um, we have you for half an hour, so
what I might ask is if you would, um, introductory, uh,
comments, introduce yourselves
and, uh, from there we'll go to question.
Thank you very much for the welcome, Tina Kote.
I'm Kelly C***s, secretary for Woman
and Chief Executive of the Ministry for Women.
I'm Anna Charmers. I'm Deputy Secretary Engagement Policy
and Delivery for Ministry for Women.
Kylie Fletcher.
Um, Kylie Fletcher.
And I lead the crown response
for Thank you.
Would you like Yeah, please if you open statement.
Open statement, please. Great. Thank you.
Um, well, thank you for the opportunity
to be here this afternoon.
Um, we appreciate the time to talk to you about, um,
the previous 12 months.
Over this period, we have continued
to advance work on a number of key women, uh,
key issues for women.
We've led initiatives focusing on closing the gender pay
gap, addressing online harm,
supporting more women into governance and leadership.
We've also contributed to a range of policy processes,
including stalking our latest stock take on public sector
boards and committees show
that women's representation in public sector governance
remains strong with women holding 52.1%
of public sector board roles
and 44.5% of chair positions.
The gender pay gap continues to impact women
to support meaningful change.
We've launched the gender pay gap toolkit with businesses
and others that we work with in November
to provide a standardized calculator for,
to calculate the gender pay gap.
We've worked with more than 300
business leaders from across the country over this time
to both promote and roll out the toolkit.
And just over a week ago,
we Lynch launched the second iteration of the toolkit,
which brings in gender and ethnicity and gender disability.
We've also, um, provided more resources for small
and medium businesses who want to do work in this area
to support them with doing that.
And we've brought forward resources to amplify the voice
of young women in this process.
Another highlight has been, uh, launching the Free
to Lead initiative to support women
and their employers to engage online with confidence.
This was developed with NetSafe
and includes modules for both employers and for,
and for women to provide practical plans
and support so that women continue, um, engaging, lying
on your own terms.
Regarding women's health
and wellbeing, we have worked
to increase the awareness of women's health.
This includes, um, work
that we released in October on menopause
and perimenopause
with a focus on supporting women in workplaces.
We've continued to support claimant participation in the Man
Kopa inquiry alongside the Ang Tribunal
process, um, is underway in public hearings will continue
through 2026.
Our international work to promote
and, um, protect the rights of women
and girls has included New Zealand participation
participating in New Zealand's CAW examination
at the UN in Geneva in October 24.
Despite significant progress, challenges do remain
and I look forward to continuing to progress work
against all of our strategic priority areas
to realize our vision
that New Zealand is a pla great place for women and girls.
Thank you very much. Thank
You very much. Uh, first,
um,
Thank you so much for being here, um, on page 13
of the annual report, and thank you for that as well.
It talks about the MINISTERIALS priorities set
by the Minister for Women, which include
lifting women's income by addressing the gender pay gap
and supporting women into work.
So from your perspective, does the ministry consider
that the delay of 33 p equity claims affecting
around 150,000 women poses a risk
to achieving its economic empowerment, priority
to lift women's incomes
and potentially undermines related work programs such
as the gen gender pay gap toolkit that you've been, um,
celebrating today?
Women's trades initiatives
and support for labor market pathways and monitoring.
And I have a few side Aries as well. Thank you. Sure.
Thank you for the question.
Um, so women's economic empowerment, um, contains a number
of components and you've, um, touched on a number of them,
um, including pay equity, uh, the gender pay,
and a whole lot of other factors too,
including those pipelines of women into, um,
being represented right through organizations.
Um, the ministry wasn't involved in the pay equity work,
but we have been focused on the gender pay gap, um, in terms
of really supporting women's economic empowerment.
We've also been working with the retirement commissioner
on the retirement savings gap also, which is another,
it's sort of where it all culminates.
Mm-hmm. So we have been, um, very focused on, um,
what we can do to support, um, organizations, um,
calculating their gap, understanding it,
and then really importantly taking action.
Um, that has been our focus over the last 12 to 18 months.
Fantastic. So does the
ministry's current monitoring indicate that changes
to pay equity progress could slow
or reverse gains in closing gender pay gaps?
Are you monitoring that?
Uh, we are not, um, currently monitoring that in terms of,
um, but we, we do, um, we'll await
with interest when claims do come through,
but what we do monitor is, um, the gender pay gap in terms
of, um, the reporting that Stats New Zealand does.
Mm-hmm. So that has been in place since 1998.
Um, so we've got a pretty good time series of that now.
Um, our current level, um,
at the national level is 5.2%.
Mm-hmm. We are
however interested in getting beneath that number as well.
So another thing that we do is look to, um, understand
what the pay gap is for Wni Maori, for Pacific Women,
and for other, um, groups of women,
including disabled women.
So we, we come at things from that perspective
as well in terms of what we are monitoring
and how we are seeing that flow through to, um, change.
So has, thank you. Um, that sounds great.
Does, has the ministry provided any advice
or analysis
to the minister outlining the potential consequences
for women's economic security arising from the changes
to pay equity claims?
Uh, um, as I touched on earlier, our, our main
contribution here in terms
of we women's economic empowerment has been really focused
on the pay gap
and we do provide, um, analysis and breakdown.
Um, we know that this, um,
that this measure has been quite stuck since 2020, sorry,
since 2017.
The gender pay gap at the national level has been really
sticky, and that was one of the main reasons, um, I guess
that there was the parliamentary inquiry into pay
transparency back in 20 21, 22.
From that, there's been quite a lot
of work underway in this space
and why we wanted to do something to really sort
of get into change here.
Um, so the, the two most recent data points in terms
of looking at the gender pay gap globally, um,
when the current number is 5.2, the year before was 8.2.
So it's a three percentage point drop, which is, um,
a significant it is and one data point.
So we do await with interest to see
what the next one will be.
Mm-hmm. Um, so it, so it's sort
of understanding what's been going on through there
that's driven that change.
Yeah. That's also, I've just got one
more supplementary Thank you.
You, and so you are confident with
what the ministry is doing within its existing mandate
to mitigate or highlight the risks, these changes pose
to women's long term income and economic wellbeing? Yeah,
So what we are viewing, um,
women's economic empowerment holistically, um,
and, um, for example, another area
that we are interested in is the fact
that women are currently getting 3% of the pie in terms of,
um, um, invest investment into new businesses despite, um,
their businesses being successful.
So that again, speaks to opportunities to, um,
advance women's economic, um, wellbeing
and, um, empowerment.
We've got the PA parts of that picture
that we are focused on,
and other government agencies are making their contribution
in other areas as well.
Thank you. I thought
Yeah, sure. What was asked. Yeah,
sure.
Um, just on the gender pay gap, um,
what analysis has the ministry done on how much of
that reduction in the pay gap is due to, uh,
collective agreements and pay equity settlements? Mm.
That's something that we haven't fully unpicked.
I think it's fair to say that there's a range
of factors at play there
and also, um, how the pay equity settlements have been, um,
impacting on that over time, rather than just this
last data point.
We do know that over the most recent period, one
of the changes, um, that came through in those numbers was
that the median, um, increase, um,
for men was much smaller than the increase for women in the,
uh, median earnings over that time, which led to, um,
the closing of the gap. So then are
You intending to do an analysis to,
to work out why there was, why there was a, a,
a shift in the gender pay gap?
Uh, y yes. Um,
and we'd have to, um, draw on the, um, the, the input
of other agencies, including Stats New Zealand.
Um, it, I think it is worth reiterating
that this is one data point.
So, um, over that time series, since 98,
we have seen a couple of periods of, um, decline.
There have also been, um, a couple of periods
where the gender pay gap has gone back up, like I said,
since 2017, that the key standout feature has been
that it just got a bit stuck.
So this, the gap,
the most recent data coming down is positive,
but it is that one data point.
So we need to see what will happen from there.
Um, going back to the original question posed
around pay equity that KA put, when we had your
minister here last time, she confirmed,
and I think you've reiterated that she never sought
advice from the Ministry for Women on pay equity changes.
Um, given that she didn't ask you this question, I will, um,
do you agree with the Minister for Women
and some of the other ministers
that the government's pay equity changes
are fairer for women?
I think that that's, um, a question, um, for my minister.
I, we haven't been,
You've got a level of expertise on pay equity, um,
and we're really grateful that your ministry has that.
So clearly you would have an evidence base and,
and important experience and insights.
Mm-hmm. So my question to you is, is as the lead
of the agency, are the changes there are,
I I do, um, acknowledge that this is a, a topic
where there are a range of views.
Um, I have, um, listened to, um, many
of those viewpoints of women leaders,
women's organizations expressed through the, um,
the People Select committee process into pay equity.
Um, and I'm connected into a range of groups who, um,
speak to that, speak to us about that
and their perspectives.
My focus really has been on what is the ministry, um,
for women that we can do, um, um, given the,
our mandate in terms of, um, we not being part
of the pay equity, um, changes that we're seen through,
but what we can do on the other side of the equation
to get the results that we all want,
which is women's economic empowerment.
Okay. One more s to that
and, um, you know,
just a little bit concerned, that was quite a vague answer,
but, you know, also concerned that according
to the public service census, only 54%
of your staff feel you are free
and frank in your advice to ministers.
Yeah. Were you free
and frank in your advice on pay equity, uh,
even though it would've been after the fact
'cause the announcement and decisions were made without your
input, were you free and,
and Frank with your advice on pay equity
and what was that advice?
Um, our focus,
um, has not, I guess, been looking back
and retrospective on that.
We've wanted to focus on what we can do
to support women's economic empowerment.
We've got the opportunity in the space of, um,
gender pay gap reporting, uh, working
with the retirement commission, working
with others on other aspects
that have the same outcome in terms
of our economic wellbeing.
And that's where we've put our effort.
So no advice no from you on pay equity to the minister?
Not since that decision was made. No.
Well, I a supplement from the pay gap, actually, sort of
what you were saying earlier, because, um,
I think the records of go back to 1998 is, as you said, um,
and, uh, the last sort of four this year is it's prop to,
uh, 5.2% that gender pay gap, which, um, my understanding
that's the lowest it's ever been since Rickles began
back in 1998.
Um, and, and you, you talked a little bit
before about, you know, you don't have all the analysis as
to why that is, but it, it, so are there any, um, indicators
that you've had yet about, you know, why it's dropped
to the last, it's been since 19 nine? Yeah,
So, um, we think
that there's a few different things going on.
Um, the pay equity settlements will be part of that,
as they would've been in previous data points as well.
Um, the, the fact that men's median earnings, the rate
of increase was quite small, um,
and women's media earnings over that period was, um, um,
quite a lot larger.
There's also, um, the work that businesses
and organizations have had underway
for some time now about pay gap reporting,
pay transparency more broadly.
Um, so for example, the champions for Change,
a grouping of around, um, 80 chief executives
and board chairs covering about 160,000 people.
Uh, they have been reporting on this for,
for quite a long time now.
And also a whole range of other organizations
that we connected with, um, are also stepping towards, um,
being more transparent about their pay gaps, um,
taking action, um, to address them if they have them.
Um, and that's part of the work we've been doing, um,
over the course of this year as we've looked
to implement this.
Um, so I think it's a number of things, um, coming together.
Um, when we look internationally, our, um,
gender pay gap is, um, is quite low.
I think we are fifth in the OECD,
but that's based on 2023 data.
So it might have shifted a bit further since then.
Um, but there's still more to go.
And as I touched on earlier, um, different groups of women,
um, are their rates, their gender pay gaps are much higher,
and that's what we want to keep focusing on as well.
And related to that last year, um,
release the first government, um,
back gender pay gap calculator
or who are wanting to address this, um, what have you,
what have you seen in the period in review in terms
of the uptake of that and,
and what, if any, difference making?
There's quite a lot of momentum behind the work.
So, um, a big, um,
a big ask from the business sector was they wanted a
standardized approach,
which is why we developed the calculator.
So it really helps simplify what gets included
and who gets included.
So that's taken away quite a big barrier.
Um, and, um, it's, it also means that organizations are able
to make comparisons, um, uh, with their peers
or across sectors, which again, is something
that they've wanted to do.
So that really enables them to do that.
We've also had interest from small and medium enterprises.
Our guidance is that you need to have at least 10 men
or 10 women, um, in order to report publicly.
It doesn't mean that you can't do the work, um,
within your business and use it, but,
but in terms of protecting people's privacy,
you can't publish it.
But I think that that level of interest again, um, speaks
to the fact that a number of organizations, um,
not just large ones,
but also smaller ones are wanting to do this.
A number of them speak about the impact
that it has on the organizational performance, um,
and the difference that it's making to them.
Um, and their, their success
Was encouraging. Um,
I've
Got primary, I wanna really come back to this issue
of the decision being made in the middle
of the year you weren't consulted.
I understand that it's difficult,
but what have you done this year to make sure
that you are measuring the impact of
that particular decision?
Because that is your role.
You are the guardrails, you are the advocate.
So what have you, what have you got in place to make sure
that next year when we look at it, we know
what impact that has had?
Um, this is something that, um, the Minister
for Women's National Advisory Council on Employment Outcomes
for Women, um, have been speaking with the minister about,
and it's something that in the context of their work
that they might be providing advice to her on.
Also, um, as I've said, um, at the ministry, we will be, um,
um, continuing to, uh, work with mb, um, as required,
but also to keep, um, oversight of, um, settlements
or, um, applications that come through this process.
Any question, ma? Oh, yes. Thank you. Um, is this
A software primary? Primary?
Oh, I've got a primary too.
I thought I was after Kahu. Oh, I'd ask sub.
You have had, uh, six sub I I'll,
I'll come back to you after this one. Okay.
Um, I'd like to come to the, um, leadership
and governance role,
and particularly the outcome that we've seen
with exceeding the target.
We are now 52.1% in the public sector.
Um, have you got any plans
or strategies of, so that
that can be translated into the private sector as well?
Thank you. And yes, we do.
So, um, it's the fifth consecutive year in the public sector
that, that we've now been at 50% or above.
Um, we've got, um, the public, the private sector, sorry, is
around, um, 36% or, or so.
Um, we've had conversations with a number
of leaders in the private sector,
and there is, um, interest in commitment in working with us
to have a focus on that.
So that's gonna be a key focus for, for us from, from now
through till next year.
Um, we've been meeting with the IOD
and others to, um, think about what steps we can take
to support, um, aspirations in the private sector
to lift that number.
We are also, um, working on, um, a refresh
and update of the nomination service that we offer, which is
where we sort of almost, um, provide an opportunity
for women to express their interest in governance roles.
They come on our database,
and we then share with them different opportunities
that are available to them.
So we are also looking at ways in which, um, that resource
that we have could potentially be used more broadly
to support women in leadership, be it in the private sector,
the public sector, or the non-government sector.
All of it makes a difference in terms of women's, um,
representation and empowerment.
Thank you. Yeah.
Um, so, uh,
I think it was the 8th of October actually on the Pacific
Media Network interview that the Prime Minister
ruled out a potential merger or,
or disestablishment of, of the ministry for Pacific Peoples.
Um, and just wanting to check, does a potential merger
also exclude the ministry from women, women, the Ministry
for Women, um, being merged?
Yes, it does. So there was no plan prior
to the 8th of October to merge the ministry, um,
with a number of other population group agencies.
Uh, so, um, myself
and my colleagues from, um, the Ministry for Pacific Peoples
and the Ministry for Ethnic Communities as, um, a group
of three ces, we were asked by the Public Service Commission
to, um, provide them with some information.
Um, so we, um, worked collaboratively to, um, to do that.
Um, there was one, um, report that talked about, um,
you know, for each organization, our background, what we do,
how we do it, and then there was another piece of work
that looked up, um, at a continuum of options,
essentially from the status quo through to different, um,
different, um, options, um, including mergers
or bringing different bits of organizations together.
When, when we were thinking about that well, um,
and we were asked to do that
and to think about what options could look like, key drivers
for that work was around, um, you know, a key part
of our role is how we bring the voices
of women to the table.
So that was something that was front of mind.
Um, also, um, how we can, um, continue to drive
and leverage impact, uh, our ambition is high and broad,
and so we are really wanting to, to, to be, um, mindful of
how we can make that happen.
And also thinking about sustainability.
Um, so they were things
that we thought about as part of that,
Is there a plan to merge the population group agencies
or disestablish the Ministry for Women next term?
And are you doing work towards that now?
Um, what I've been advised by, um,
the Public Services Commission, um,
and actually through, through the media, through the media
that you referenced, is that there is no, no, no, no work,
nothing happening, um, at this point.
And no, I'm not doing any, um, work on that currently.
So, um, in terms of, um, some of the documents
and answers that, that you've given, um,
around this particular issue, according
to the minister's answers to written questions,
you initiated a discussion
with her on the 10th of September, 2025,
about the future of the ministry.
Uh, can you tell us what prompted the discussion and whether
or not any of that discussion included a potential merger
or a disestablishment of the ministry for women?
No, I, um, if I, um, recall correctly, I don't have the,
um, timeline, um, in front of me,
but I think that discussion was following, um, a meeting
that my minister had with the Public Service Commission.
It was a meeting that I wasn't at,
so I think I was just looping back into her, um,
looping back, um, with her after that meeting. Okay.
So final question.
You've said that there's absolutely no intention
to merge the Ministry for women
or disestablish it next term?
Uh, no, that's not what I've said,
that they're not decisions for me.
Um, they are decisions
for the Public Service Commission in terms of the machinery
of government, just
To understand. So you
haven't been part
of any discussions to do those things?
Um, we've, we
provided advice, uh, sorry, not,
it wasn't advice, it was information.
We provided information to the Public Service Commission on,
um, kind of background to our ministries, what we do,
how we operate, and we were also asked to think about, um,
different options on a continuum.
Um, I've been advised that there is nothing happening, um,
on this, um, in this term of government,
and I'm not doing any work on it. I
Was asking that next term, uh,
That not, I haven't been advised of any,
anything with that regard.
All I've heard is what you've repeated
that you heard in the media.
Um, but, um,
We've done a lot of work in economically local and so on.
And one of the things that comes
through internationally strongly is that regardless of
what level women in entrepreneurship is one
of the key sources of increasing that, both generally
for women's empowerment,
but also for the communities they work in
and the sectors they work in.
So I was a little disappointed when I saw that, um,
we weren't really getting the investment into some
of our entrepreneurs that we should be getting.
So can you, um, please sort of outline to us
what those challenges seem to be
and what the opportunities may be to up the ante?
Yes. Um, thank you for that question.
Well, it, it does seem that getting 3% of that pie, um,
yeah, it, it's not, um, where we wanna be.
And I think for the entrepreneurs, the women, it's not what,
where they want things to be at either.
So we've, we've sort of at the point
of starting some conversations with, um, groups, um,
working in this space, including the Ministry of Awesome

and really starting to think about how we can unpack
what some of the barriers are.
I think a really good thing is
that this number is out there now that people are aware
of it, um, that that's something that's being, uh, watched
and monitored and getting a conversation going.
Um, but we are really keen to work with others
to free up the space.
Um, like you, you, the point you made too is
that we know from research that women led organizations,
well, firstly, they, um, often have small agenda pay gaps.
So, um, that's one thing that they do,
but also they, um, are great at bringing in
and promoting women within their organization.
So I think it ticks a lot of boxes
and it's something that we wanna do
more work on in the year ahead. Mm-hmm.
So The quick stop on that one.

commercializing university, uh, research

That's something, um, to completely honest,
be completely honest that I haven't, um,
thought a lot of about.
Um, but we could come back to you, um, in writing, if

You. I've

For administrator, sort of a partner with anyone on that, in

and in women entrepreneurship.

And, um, there's a range of players, um,
or people leading out in this area.

to do on issues like this is really looking to, um,
connect people, bring people together, um,
to support good work that's already going on,

So we are really up to that part of this piece of work,
and we will be working with a range of leaders, um,

So hopefully, um, in 12 months time,
we'll be back here talking to you about the progress

is a woman bit noisy, the, the president

you know, I was visiting a John Kong.

and recently, uh, producing a lot of, um,
well world leading, um, products for, you know,
space companies, um, also lead by, you know,
female leadership team.
Um, so it's just,

Or is there, is there a process for,

Yeah, so, so a lot more young girls can see themselves

Um, we've got, in that case, it's sort of, um, a range

that I think the, the, like, the message

Um, also on our online harm work, we've got a series

And also, um, throughout all those stories is, is a message
of encouragement
and to keep going, to keep being visible,
to own your space, to have your voice.

We've got, um, aspirations to continue with the, um,
the opportunity for people to hear

Um, there's a, a range of issues, um, that we need
to come from, from different perspectives

so we are hearing from community organizations about the

building on the measures that we've als already got,

source_metadata.description:
Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee

2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Women

Hearing of evidence:

Ministry for Women

Kellie Coombes, Chief Executive – Secretary for Women  
Anna Chalmers, Deputy Chief Executive, Engagement, Policy and Delivery 
Kylie Fletcher, Kaiwhakahaere, Mana Wāhine Kaupapa Inquiry 
Nadine Millin, Chief People Office 
Catherine Williams, Strategic Advisor to the Chief Executive
